en.extract:
The wood snipe (Gallinago nemoricola) is a species of snipe which breeds in the Himalayas of northern India, Nepal, Bhutan and southern China. In winter, it occurs at lower altitudes in the Himalayas, as a regular visitor in small numbers to north Vietnam. it also occurs as a vagrant in central and southern India, Sri Lanka, Bangladesh, Myanmar, north Thailand and Laos. It is reportedly very well-known to Chauri herders in the Himalayas.
This is a dark snipe, 28–32 centimetres (11–13 in) in length, with a short, broad-based bill. In the May-July breeding season, it breeds in alpine meadows above 3,000 metres (9,800 ft), moving to lower altitudes in the winter.  Wood snipes breed and nest on boulders near rhododendron shrubs. A 2021 study in Sichuan province during the breeding season (May-July), showed that  the birds preferred the lower parts (3,378–3,624 m) of the alpine meadow with intermediate levels of soil moisture, with the birds preferring to forage at sites with higher soil fauna abundance. 
The species has been  classified as vulnerable by IUCN, with a population of mature birds between 2500 and 10,000. The widespread loss of wetlands habitats in its breeding and wintering grounds has been a major reason for its decline. It has been recorded in some protected areas, including Langtang and Sagarmatha National Parks in Nepal.
It eats invertebrates, primarily worms. Its diet also includes seeds and larvae found in Chauri dung.


Taxonomy
It was first described in 1836 by Brian Houghton Hodgson.


References


External links
Occurrence data for Gallinago nemoricola from GBIF

[[File:Wood Snipe, Thailand.jpg|thumb|Wood Snipe, [[Thailand]]]]
The '''wood snipe''' ('''''Gallinago nemoricola''''') is a species of [[snipe]] which breeds in the [[Himalayas]] of northern [[India]], [[Nepal]], [[Bhutan]] and southern [[China]]. In winter, it occurs at lower altitudes in the Himalayas, as a regular visitor in small numbers to north [[Vietnam]]. it also occurs as a vagrant in central and southern India, [[Sri Lanka]], [[Bangladesh]], [[Myanmar]], north [[Thailand]] and [[Laos]]. It is reportedly very well-known to Chauri herders in the Himalayas.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last1=Basnet |first1=Hari |last2=Shrestha |first2=Mohan Bikram |last3=Thakuri |first3=Deelip Chand |last4=Pun |first4=Tejab |last5=Chaudhary |first5=Dhiraj |last6=Baral |first6=Hem Sagar |date=2021-11-11 |title=Ecology and status of Wood Snipe Gallinago nemoricola in Lamtang National Park, Nepal |journal=Wader Study |volume=128 |issue=3 |doi=10.18194/ws.00243 |issn=2058-8410}}</ref>

This is a dark snipe, {{convert|28|–|32|cm}} in length, with a short, broad-based bill. In the May-July breeding season, it breeds in [[alpine tundra|alpine meadow]]s above {{convert|3,000|m}}, moving to lower altitudes in the winter.  Wood snipes breed and nest on boulders near [[rhododendron]] shrubs.<ref name=":0" /> A 2021 study in [[Sichuan|Sichuan province]] during the breeding season (May-July), showed that  the birds preferred the lower parts (3,378–3,624 m) of the alpine meadow with intermediate levels of soil moisture, with the birds preferring to forage at sites with higher soil fauna abundance.<ref>{{Cite Q|Q122705752}}</ref> 

The species has been  classified as vulnerable by IUCN, with a population of mature birds between 2500 and 10,000.<ref name="iucn status 12 November 2021"/> The widespread loss of wetlands habitats in its breeding and wintering grounds has been a major reason for its decline.<ref name="iucn status 12 November 2021"/> It has been recorded in some protected areas, including [[Langtang]] and [[Sagarmatha National Park]]s in Nepal.

It eats [[Invertebrate|invertebrates]], primarily worms.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=Faheem |first=Mahmood Syed |title=Wood snipe |url=https://indianbirds.thedynamicnature.com/2017/06/wood-snipe-gallinago-nemoricola.html |access-date=2023-09-08}}</ref> Its diet also includes seeds<ref name=":1" /> and larvae found in Chauri dung.<ref name=":0" />
==Taxonomy==
It was first described in 1836 by [[Brian Houghton Hodgson]].<ref name=hodgson>{{cite journal|page=8|author-link= Brian Houghton Hodgson| author=Hodgson, B.H| title=January 26, 1836|journal=Proceedings of the Zoological Society of London |issue=Pt. 4 No. 37 |date= 1836| url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/30570732}}</ref>

zh.extract:
林沙锥（学名：Gallinago nemoricola）为鹬科沙锥属的鸟类。在中国大陆，分布于云南等地。该物种的模式产地在尼泊尔。


特征
林沙锥体重约167.67克，翼长约135毫米，嘴峰长约71.4毫米，喙宽度约6.1毫米，喙厚度约8.9毫米，跗蹠长约34.4毫米，尾长约53.8毫米。林沙锥是候鸟，其栖息在开放的高大树木组成的森林中，食性为肉食性，主要食物来源是水生动物。


分布
繁殖于喜马拉雅山脉和西藏；在印度和东南亚地区越冬。
